coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for the detection of imidacloprid residue in leafy vegetables
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Chunhao
Guo, Wenchuan
coredata.dc:description: Consumption of leafy vegetables with excessive imidacloprid (IMI) can cause serious harm to the human body. To achieve rapid IMI detection, a carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent (FL) probe was hydrothermally prepared using O-phenylenediamine as the precursor. The morphology, particle size distribution, crystal structure, optics and chemical bond state of the as-prepared CDs were characterized. The mechanism of the CDs in detecting IMI was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and the CDs’ selectivity, stability, sensitivity, and actual sample recovery were tested. The CDs showed good selectivity, stability, and anti-interference ability. Under optimum conditions, there was a strong linear relationship between the FL intensity of the CDs and the IMI concentration in the range of 0.037–0.2 mg/L. The detection limit was 0.00187 mg/kg. The CDs were successfully applied to detect IMI in lettuce, cole, spinach, and pakchoi with spiked recoveries between 81.026% and 106.803% and a relative standard deviation between 0.001 and 0.027%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pesticide residue detection
Leafy vegetables
O-phenylenediamine
Imidacloprid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.31772065). The authors would like to thank Meiling Song, Menghan Sun, and Xiaoqin Wen from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their assistance with the SEM, TEM, and XPS analyses, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process and detection principle of CDs with OPD as the precursor.
(A) SEM image, (B) TEM image, (C) particle size distribution, and (D) UV absorption spectrum of the CDs.

(A) FL excitation and emission spectra of CDs (inset: photos of CDs under excitation of unexcited and 365 nm light sources). (B) FL excitation stacking spectra of CDs at 285 to 405 nm. (C) XRD spectrum and (D) EDX spectrum of the CDs.
Survey XPS spectrum (A), high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1S(B), N1S(C), O1S(D) of CDs.
FTIR transmission spectra of CDs, CDs + IMI, and IMI.
Results of the IMI spiked assay in actual samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for the detection of imidacloprid residue in leafy vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical and Electronic Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical and Electronic Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Internet of Things, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Internet of Things
Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Internet of Things, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Agricultural Information Perception and Intelligent Service, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Agricultural Information Perception and Intelligent Service

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Agricultural Information Perception and Intelligent Service, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Consumption of leafy vegetables with excessive imidacloprid (IMI) can cause serious harm to the human body. To achieve rapid IMI detection, a carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent (FL) probe was hydrothermally prepared using O-phenylenediamine as the precursor. The morphology, particle size distribution, crystal structure, optics and chemical bond state of the as-prepared CDs were characterized. The mechanism of the CDs in detecting IMI was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and the CDs’ selectivity, stability, sensitivity, and actual sample recovery were tested. The CDs showed good selectivity, stability, and anti-interference ability. Under optimum conditions, there was a strong linear relationship between the FL intensity of the CDs and the IMI concentration in the range of 0.037–0.2 mg/L. The detection limit was 0.00187 mg/kg. The CDs were successfully applied to detect IMI in lettuce, cole, spinach, and pakchoi with spiked recoveries between 81.026% and 106.803% and a relative standard deviation between 0.001 and 0.027%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are widely used for controlling various pests in vegetable cultivation but can lead to residual pesticide presence in the harvested products (Si et al., 2021). Among all types of vegetables, leafy vegetables are the most commonly exposed to pesticides. Imidacloprid (IMI), a common carbamate pesticide, is widely used in pest control applications. The consumption of vegetables containing excessive IMI residue can cause rhabdomyolysis, kidney damage, metabolic encephalopathy, ventricular fibrillation, and multiorgan failure, potentially leading to death in severe cases (Bhatta et al., 2023). Thus, detecting IMI residue in leafy vegetables is vital for ensuring vegetable safety. Current methods for detecting IMI include enzyme immunoassays (Wu et al., 2020), gas chromatography (Tamdjo Tache et al., 2023), and visible/near-infrared spectroscopy (Yu et al., 2022). While enzyme immunoassays and gas chromatography offer high detection accuracy, these methods are complex, requiring expensive equipment or specialized personnel (Chu et al., 2020; Han et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2021). Visible/near-infrared spectroscopy offers the advantage of rapid detection but suffers from low sensitivity and is unsuitable for trace detection. Therefore, developing a rapid, convenient, and sensitive method for detecting IMI residues in vegetables is an urgent priority.

In recent years, substantial efforts have been focused on developing rapid detection methodologies for pesticide residues. Zhang et al. (2020b) proposed a method with multiwalled carbon nanotubes as dispersive solid-phase extraction sorbents coupled with surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) for detecting deltamethrin. The best performing prediction model was established with partial least squares regression of the SERS spectra ranges of 545–573 cm−1 and 987–1011 cm−1 with a coefficient of determination (R 2) of 0.9306, a detection limit of 0.484 mg/L and a residual predictive deviation of 3.046. For the rapid detection of acetamiprid (ACE) residues in vegetables, Zhu et al. (2022) established an indirect competitive chemiluminescence enzyme immunoassay (ic-CLEIA). The detection range and the detection limit of ic-CLEIA were 0.70–96.31 and 0.70 ng/mL, respectively. ic-CLEIA had the advantages of a simple pretreatment and detection process, good sensitivity and accuracy and was found to meet the needs of rapid screening of ACE residues in vegetables. Further application of the enzyme immunoassay was found in the work of Zhai et al. (2023), who established a colorimetric enzyme-linked immunoassay assay (Co-ELISA) and chemiluminescence enzyme-linked immunoassay assay (Cl-ELISA) to detect IMI residues in vegetables. The linear range of Co-ELISA ranged between 1.56 μg/L and 200 μg/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.56 μg/L. The values for Cl-ELISA were 0.19 μg/L to 25 μg/L with an LOD of 0.19 μg/L. To rapidly detect cyromazine residues, Peng et al. (2022) prepared a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor to detect cyromazine residues in agricultural products. Tomato, cowpea, and water samples were tested for cyromazine recovery rate. The concentration of cyromazine showed a strong linear relationship with the peak response current of the sensor developed in this study. The lower detection limit for cyromazine was 0.5 µmol/L, and the sensor also had good reproducibility and interference resistance. Kim et al. (2018) developed a paper-based sensor for chlorpyrifos, achieving detection of the pesticide within 5 min with an LOD of 8.60 ppm. However, the above methods require complex sensor preparation and are costly.

Quantum dots can overcome the defects of the above detection methods and have good optical and electrical properties (He et al., 2022). However, nontoxic quantum dots are urgently needed to overcome this drawback due to the high toxicity of heavy metal-based quantum dots, which poses a potential threat to both detectors and the environment (Zhou et al., 2021). As a new type of nontoxic quantum dots, in recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have become a popular research topic (Li & Zhang, 2021; Zhang, Hu, Wu, & Chen, 2020a; Zhong et al., 2023). Due to their easy fabrication, good chemical inertness, resistance to photobleaching, and low cost (Xu et al., 2019), CDs have become widely used in the field of food safety (Liang et al., 2022; Vijeata et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2020). Fluorescence (FL) methods based on CDs, which offer the advantages of high efficiency, rapid speed, low cost, and high sensitivity, are widely used to detect pesticide residues (Wu et al., 2022). CDs-based sensors rely on the interaction between the reactive groups on their surfaces and those of the analyte, resulting in FL electron transfer and thus inferring the analyte content by detecting changes in FL intensity or lifetime (Yang et al., 2019a; Yang et al., 2022). To the best of the authors’ knowledge, few analytical methods exist that utilize CDs to detect IMI residue rapidly. Therefore, this study aims to develop a CDs-based FL sensor to detect IMI residues in leafy vegetables. The specific objectives include (1) preparing an efficient, rapid, highly sensitive, stable, and cost-effective CDs-based FL probe for IMI residue detection, (2) characterizing the synthesized CDs thoroughly, and (3) analysing the comprehensive performance of the prepared CDs.

The chemicals used in this study, including magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), glucose, sucrose, phenylalanine, citric acid, ascorbic acid, and O-phenylenediamine (OPD) (AR, 98%), were produced from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). IMI was produced by Shaanxi Sunger Road Bio-science Co., Ltd (Xi’an, Shaanxi, China). Thiacloprid was produced by Hebei Guanlong Agrochemical Co., Ltd (Baoding, Hebei, China). Nitenpyram was produced by Shaanxi Biaozheng Crop Science Co., Ltd (Weinan, Shaanxi, China). Acetamiprid was produced by Shaanxi Huarongkaiwei Biological Co., Ltd (Xi’an, Shaanxi, China). The vegetables used for the study, namely lettuce, cole, spinach, and pakchoi, were purchased from a local vegetable market (Yangling, Shaanxi, China). The dialysis tubing was produced by Hunan Yibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Changsha, Hunan, China). Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·12H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·2H2O), and other necessary chemical reagents were produced by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All water used in the experiments was ultrapure. All the other reagents were analytical grade and used directly.

The morphological characteristics and energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) of CDs were analysed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Sigma 300, Zeiss, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was acquired with a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The particle size distribution of CDs was examined through dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a nano-laser particle sizer (ZEN3600, Malvern Instruments Limited, UK). The crystalline structure of CDs was studied using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (D8 Avance A25, Bruker, Germany), with the sweep angle set to range from 10 to 60 degrees. The functional groups of CDs, IMI, and the CDs + IMI complex were explored through a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) instrument (Vertex 70, Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a K-Alpha+ (Thermo Scientific, USA) with peak positions being recalibrated by shifting C 1 s peak to 284.8 eV. CDs’ FL intensity and spectra were evaluated with an FL spectrophotometer (LS55, Perkin-Elmer, USA), and the excitation and emission slits were set to 10 nm. CDs’ ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectra were measured using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan). The detailed characterization method (SEM, TEM, DLS, XRD, FTIR, and XPS) can be found in the supplementary materials.

CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method (Kumar et al., 2022). The steps were as follows: 0.2 g of OPD was dissolved in 40 mL of water and sonicated (40 kHz, 20 °C) for 15 min. Then, 40 mL of water was added and sonicated for another 15 min. The OPD solution was then transferred to an autoclave with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and reacted at 120 °C for 4 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, a yellow–brown liquid product was obtained. Afterwards, the obtained product was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 15 min, followed by filtering (0.22 μm) to separate the large particles and dialysis tubing (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove small particles. Finally, the CDs were dried in a vacuum freeze dryer and weighed on an electronic balance to configure a standard solution, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further experiments. The preparation process and detection principle of CDs are shown in Fig. 1 . Under the irradiation of an excitation light source, the IMI solution and CDs were mixed, resulting in the absorption of the FL emitted by CDs by IMI, leading to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and subsequent FL quenching. The content of IMI was then calculated based on the FL intensity of the mixture after adding IMI.

The prepared CDs emitted FL after being irradiated by the excitation light source, and the FL intensity decreased after adding a certain amount of IMI. A standard detection curve was constructed by fitting the FL intensity of the CDs to the IMI content. The standard curve was used to calculate the IMI content after measuring the FL intensity of the CDs after adding IMI.

To enhance the analytical performance of the CDs, we investigated the impact of IMI amount on the FL intensity of the detection system. Specifically, varying volumes of IMI (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 600 μL) were added to a cuvette containing 1 mL of the CDs solution, and the resulting FL intensity was measured.

To investigate the effect of pH on the results of the IMI content assay, phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with different pH values were prepared with 0.1 mol/L NaH2PO4·12H2O, Na2HPO4·2H2O, hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solutions. CDs solutions with different pH values were obtained by mixing the highly concentrated CDs solutions with PBS buffer at different pH values.

The equilibration time of the reaction between CDs and IMI is an important indicator of the performance of CDs. To investigate the equilibrium time of the reaction between CDs and IMI, we mixed 300 μL of IMI solution with 1 mL of CDs solution in the cuvette, and the FL intensity was detected by an FL spectrophotometer every 30 s for 12 min after the addition, and the results were recorded.

The long-term stability of CDs is an important indicator of their performance. To test the long-term stability of the prepared CDs, it was necessary to measure the recombination efficiency of the CDs within a few days of preparation. Therefore, the FL intensity of the CDs was continuously monitored for 12 days after the addition of IMI at concentrations of 0.063, 0.071, and 0.077 mg/L.

FRET will occur after CDs and IMI are mixed, causing FL quenching. However, substances similar to IMI interferents can decrease the sensitivity and selectivity of the CDs, affecting assay results. CDs’ sensitivity and selectivity were tested by adding common interferents (thiacloprid, nitenpyram, acetamiprid, glucose, sucrose, phenylalanine, citric acid, ascorbic acid, and halide solutions with Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+) to CDs solutions. With an IMI concentration of 0.263 mg/L and interferents at 250 mg/L, 300 μL of each interferent was added to 1 mL of CDs solution and then mixed thoroughly, and the FL intensity was measured after 6 min.

The standard detection curve was constructed by preparing IMI solutions of varying concentrations (0.037–0.2 mg/L). Initially, 1.0 g of IMI solid powder was weighed using an electronic balance and dissolved in 1000 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). Subsequently, a 1 mg/L IMI stock solution was obtained by diluting 1 mL of this solution to 1000 mL. Finally, IMI solutions within the concentration range of 0.037–0.2 mg/L were prepared by incrementally adjusting the volume of 1 mL of the stock solution to 5–27 mL and storing them in brown bottles.

The emission wavelength of the FL spectrophotometer was set to 365 nm. A mixture of 300 μL IMI solution and 1 mL CDs solution was prepared in a cuvette and thoroughly shaken for 6 min, and the FL intensity and spectrum were measured. This process was repeated three times for each IMI concentration, and the average value was calculated. The IMI concentration and FL intensity data were subjected to linear fitting, resulting in the construction of a standard detection curve.

The potential of the prepared CDs to detect IMI residue in real samples was evaluated by measuring the IMI content in lettuce, cole, spinach, and pakchoi through a spiked experiment. The samples were washed with ultrapure water and dried naturally. The amounts of IMI added to the real samples were 0.00167, 0.001, and 0.0004 mg. The procedure was as follows: First, 2.0 g of IMI solid powder was weighed and dissolved in 1000 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). Subsequently, 1 mL of the solution was diluted to 1000 mL, yielding a 2 mg/L IMI solution. Finally, 3 mL aliquots of the IMI solution (2 mg/L) were individually diluted to 1.2, 2, and 5 mL, resulting in IMI solutions of 1.67, 1, and 0.4 mg/L, respectively. Two gram samples of lettuce, cole, spinach, and pakchoi were weighed using an electronic balance. One millilitre of the IMI solutions with concentrations of 1.67, 1, and 0.4 mg/L were taken and sprayed on the surface of the samples. The processing steps were repeated three times for each sample, and the samples were air-dried for 72 h (25 ± 2 °C, 25% relative humidity, no wind) for further experiments.

The air-dried samples and 10 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) were placed in a triangular flask and shaken for 2 min. A 300 μL mixture was added to a cuvette containing 1 mL of the standard CDs solution, after which the FL intensity was measured using a spectrophotometer. Each sample was measured in parallel three times. The mean values of the FL intensities were substituted into the equations of the constructed standard detection curve, and the recoveries and RSD of the actual samples were calculated.

The SEM image of the prepared CDs is presented in Fig. 2 A. The dehydrated CDs exhibited a uniform particle size, confirming the successful preparation of CDs. We hypothesized that these CDs would exhibit an amorphous structure akin to the morphology of graphene, aligning with findings from previous studies (Alvandi et al., 2021; Song et al., 2021). Fig. 2B displays the TEM image of the synthesized CDs. The CDs were predominantly spherical, with particle sizes primarily ranging approximately 10 nm.

The DLS characterization of the CDs is displayed in Fig. 2C, and the particle size of the CDs appeared to be less than 10 nm with a single peak, indicating a uniform particle distribution. This result is consistent with the SEM and TEM characterization results. The UV absorption spectrum of the CDs, provided in Fig. 2D, revealed two pronounced absorption peaks at 260 nm and 415 nm. These peaks were attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO and CN bonds, respectively.

Fig. 3 A presents the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. The excitation peak of FL was located at 365 nm, while the emission peak of the CDs under the excitation light was at 470 nm. When unexcited, the CDs exhibited a transparent light yellow colour, transitioning to light green under the excitation of a 365 nm light source. As shown in Fig. 3B, the emission peak of the CDs remained consistent (470 nm) despite changes in the excitation light source wavelength. This observation was likely attributable to the narrow diameter dispersion of the CDs (Yang et al., 2019b).

As shown in Fig. 3C, the XRD spectrum featured a broad peak in the 2θ range of 20-30°. This finding suggested that the CDs were primarily composed of an amorphous carbon phase, aligning with previous research (Khaledian et al., 2021). This observation also supported the hypothesis formulated from the SEM and DLS characterization earlier in this work.

Fig. 3D presents the EDX spectra of the CDs, revealing their elemental composition as 64.07% carbon (C), 21.67% nitrogen (N), and 14.26% oxygen (O). The high carbon content could be attributed to the abundance of aromatic compounds in the CDs. The nitrogen and oxygen contents were likely due to the high quantity of NH and CO bonds within the CDs.

The XPS technique is widely used for chemically characterizing CDs, providing detailed information about the elemental composition and bond properties of the atoms present. XPS spectra revealed that the as-prepared CDs primarily consisted of C, O, and N (Fig. 4 A). In the high-resolution spectrum of the C 1 s peak (Fig. 4B), there were three deconvoluted peaks at binding energies (BEs) of 284.8, 286.03, and 287.4 eV. These peaks could be attributed to different carbon species: sp2 carbon (CC/CC), oxidized carbon (CO and CO), and sp3 bonded carbon (CN). The N 1 s peak (Fig. 4C) exhibited two deconvoluted peaks at BEs of 399.61 and 400.98 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen (CN) and amino nitrogen (NH), respectively. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4D) displayed two deconvoluted peaks at BEs of 531.13 and 532.2 eV, representing CO and CO groups, respectively. These XPS results provided valuable insights into the elemental composition and bonding characteristics of the CDs under investigation.

Fig. 5 displays the FTIR transmission spectra of the surface groups of the CDs, CDs + IMI, and IMI. The FTIR transmission spectra exhibited six distinct absorption peaks at 3250–3600 cm−1, 3435 cm−1, 750 cm−1, 1632 cm−1, 1410 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1. The broad peaks in the 3250–3600 cm−1 range were likely due to the stretching vibration of OH, while the peak at 3435 cm−1 suggested the presence of NH in the CDs structure. The peak at 750 cm−1 indicated the prevalence of –NH2 groups on the surface of the CDs. The abundance of amino and hydroxyl groups could effectively enhance the CDs’ hydrophilicity, thus ensuring their water stability (Hou et al., 2016). The peaks at 1632 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 originated from the benzene ring CC bonds within the CDs structure (Đorđević et al., 2016). The peak at 1100 cm−1 resulted from the bending vibration of CH, denoting the presence of aromatic rings of OPD residues. These residues played a crucial role in IMI recognition by CDs, and were facilitated by the formation of π-π stacks.

The FTIR spectrum of the CDs + IMI combination revealed three peaks at 1598, 1515, and 1446 cm−1, suggesting the presence of aromatic rings. The peak at 1356 cm−1 was attributed to NH bending vibrations, while the peak at 1263 cm−1 was derived from CN stretching vibrations. Broad peaks from 3250 to 3600 cm−1 were associated with OH stretching vibrations. The FTIR spectrum remained largely unaltered after IMI addition, indicating the absence of covalent bonding interactions between IMI and CDs. This suggested that functional groups such as NH on IMI and –COOH on CDs interacted via noncovalent bonds, forming π-π stacking interactions that capture IMI.

The FL quenching caused by IMI was attributed to FRET caused by the overlap of the emission spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of IMI. When IMI was not present in the detection system, CDs could emit FL. However, when IMI was present in the detection system, CDs attracted IMI through π-π stacking, and FRET occurred, which reduced the FL intensity in the detection system to detect IMI content.

The FL quenching properties of CDs with various amounts of IMI are shown in Fig. S1A. It was observed that the FL intensity of the CDs varied with the introduction of IMI. Specifically, as the amount of IMI increased, the FL intensity decreased. However, the FL intensity remained stable once IMI was added to 300 μL. Therefore, 300 μL of IMI was added for subsequent measurements.

Fig. S1B and S1C show the change in the FL intensity of CDs at varying pH levels and FL intensity over reaction time, respectively. Fig. S1B illustrates that the FL intensity of the CDs escalates with increasing solution pH. Lower pH may attenuate the surface state of CDs, consequently diminishing the FL intensity, which hinders IMI residue detection. However, elevated pH levels could compromise the structural integrity of IMI components, potentially leading to inaccurate assay results (Han et al., 2019). Therefore, a pH of 7.4 was selected as the optimal assay condition in this study.

As depicted in Fig. S1C, the FL intensity of the prepared CDs dramatically diminished upon the addition of IMI, stabilizing after approximately 6 min. This observation aligned with findings reported by other studies (Li et al., 2016). Thus, a 6-min duration was adopted for FL intensity measurements in all subsequent studies.

The stability results of the CDs are shown in Fig. S1D. The FL intensity of the CDs continuously decreased over 12 days, with a decrease degree between 1.38% and 1.43%, which was within an acceptable range. This indicated that the prepared CDs have good long-term stability.

Fig. S2 indicates the impact of potential coexisting interferents on the detection results of IMI content. As shown, even when the concentration of interferents significantly exceeded that of IMI, their influence on the FL intensity of CDs was negligible. Under the excitation light source, only IMI induced a more pronounced quenching at 470 nm, reflecting the high specificity and selectivity of the sensor for IMI and affirming the robust stability of the detection system.

Fig. S3A illustrates the influence of IMI concentration on the FL intensity of CDs, with the intensity progressively decreasing as IMI concentration increased. Fig. S3B shows the relationship between FL intensity (y) and IMI concentration (x) across various IMI concentrations. For the same IMI concentration, the standard deviations of the detection results were small, and the detection system could always produce reliable and consistent results. The prepared CDs had good reproducibility, which provided a good basis for subsequent experiments. The results indicated a consistent decrease in FL intensity with an increase in IMI concentration, and the linear fitting equation between them is given in Eq. (1).
(1)
y = - 3217.6 x + 766.51

The determination coefficient of Eq. (1) was R 2 = 0.97, suggesting a high degree of correlation. This equation served as the standard detection curve, and the LOD derived from this equation was 0.00187 mg/kg. These results indicated the high feasibility of using CDs for IMI residue detection. Compared to detection methodologies proposed by other researchers (Table S1), the CDs-based FL sensor proposed in this study demonstrated high sensitivity and excellent selectivity in detection.

The performance of the prepared CDs was examined for practical applications via spiked experiments, where the IMI content of lettuce, cole, spinach, and pakchoi samples was detected. The results, as displayed in Table 1 , show that the recoveries of the spiked samples for IMI content detection in these vegetables ranged from 81.026% to 106.803%, with an RSD ranging from 0.001 to 0.027%. The high accuracy and low RSD demonstrate the practical utility of the prepared CDs for detecting IMI residues in leafy vegetables.

In this study, we prepared a CDs-based FL probe via a hydrothermal method using OPD as the precursor for the rapid detection of IMI. The resulting CDs exhibited FL excitation and emission peaks at 365 and 470 nm, respectively. Morphologically, the CDs were less than 10 nm in size, uniform in shape, and resembled graphene in the amorphous carbon phase. FTIR spectroscopy revealed the interaction between the NH functional groups on IMI and –COOH on CDs through noncovalent bonding, forming π-π stacks. This led to FL quenching by FRET, which reduced the fluorescence intensity in the detection system to detect IMI content. There was a strong linear relationship (R 2 = 0.97) between the FL intensity of the CDs and the IMI concentration in the range of 0.037–0.2 mg/L under optimum conditions. The LOD was 0.00187 mg/kg. Selectivity experiments demonstrated the robust anti-interference capability of the CDs and the high stability of the detection system. The practical application of CDs for IMI detection in lettuce, cole, spinach, and pakchoi was confirmed through spiked experiments, with recovery rates ranging between 81.026% and 106.803% and an RSD of 0.001–0.027%. In conclusion, the CDs developed in this study represent a cost-effective, efficient, stable, rapid, and highly sensitive method for detecting IMI residue in leafy vegetables, suggesting substantial potential for broader applications.

CDs have exhibited the ability to detect IMI residues rapidly and effectively as nanoscale sensor materials. Nevertheless, to meet the demands of pesticide residue detection in practical settings, it is essential to further explore and optimize the performance and structural design of CDs. This will ensure that their high accuracy and sensitivity are maintained under complex detection environments. Presently, the nonrecyclability of CDs postuse constitutes a significant limitation, and could potentially lead to secondary pollution following IMI residue detection. Thus, future research endeavours should be directed towards developing reusable CDs.

Chunhao Cao: Investigation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Wenchuan Guo: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.31772065). The authors would like to thank Meiling Song, Menghan Sun, and Xiaoqin Wen from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their assistance with the SEM, TEM, and XPS analyses, respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137578.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alvandi et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Off–on sensor based on concentration-dependent multicolor fluorescent carbon dots for detecting pesticides
Nano-Structures & Nano-Objects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alvandi, N., Assariha, S., Esfandiari, N., & Jafari, R. (2021). Off–on sensor based on concentration-dependent multicolor fluorescent carbon dots for detecting pesticides. Nano-Structures & Nano-Objects, 26, 100706. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoso.2021.100706.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bhatta et al., 2023
